vitals.date_specific: 2026: April 18 (second edition of the current RBC GranFondo-backed event)
vitals.entry_fee: From $199 USD (2026 standard price; historical promotional tiers varied)
route_options: Gran Fondo: 92km, 980m — marketed by the organizer as the '90/100km' distance

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: GOLD wave 2026-07-24 (batch C, sol-reviewed): vitals correction plus one dimension change. Research (codex/gpt-5.6-sol web search against rbcgranfondo.com, Race Roster, and Mexican/endurance-sports media, independently re-confirmed on rbcgranfondo.com/event-resources/nayarit/race-distances) confirmed the event is active and backed by the RBC GranFondo/Whistler organizing team, locally produced with Sabino Creaciones. Field size (400-500) is the producer's/organizer's own reported figure, not an independently audited count. Real 2026 course: Forte 130km/1,120m (main distance used here) and Gran Fondo 92km/980m (added as route_options) — the old 140km figure and the 2016 YouTube-sourced '165km/9 climbs/1,800m' course description both describe an unrelated, earlier same-region event; the current event's own organizer calls 2025 its inaugural edition. Field corrected to 400-500 (2026, second edition) from 'not specified'; entry fee corrected to 'from $199' from unspecified. One dimension change: logistics 4->2 (the real host area, Nuevo Vallarta/Bahia de Banderas, sits roughly 30-40 minutes from Puerto Vallarta International Airport with abundant resort lodging — a genuine resort corridor, not the remote/limited-lodging profile the old logistics=4 implied). All other dimensions left unchanged — no research basis found to adjust distance, climbing, descent_technicality, climate_risk, organization, scenic_experience, community_culture, altitude, prestige, field_depth, value, expenses, road_surface, or cultural_impact. Net dimension change: -2 (logistics). overall_score: 60 -> 57 (TIER 2 -> TIER 3, since 57 falls below the 60 T2 threshold). Original score before this wave: 64 (migrated from gravel-race-automation, pre-recalculation).

course_description.character: A paved, northbound coastal-highway fondo starting at Boulevard Nuevo Vallarta (event hub at Playa FIBBA), running through Higuera Blanca, Sayulita, Lo de Marcos and Monteon to La Pena for the 130km Forte distance. The route transitions from the Nuevo Vallarta resort zone into tropical vegetation and the protected Sierra de Vallejo landscape, passing surf and coastal communities with intermittent Pacific views. The organizer describes it as rolling terrain with short, steep climbs rather than a single marquee mountain ascent. Roads are not fully closed to traffic — the 2026 municipal notice described controlled circulation with only a temporary closure on Paseo de los Cocoteros, a real difference from a closed-highway event.
course_description.signature_challenge: The transition from resort boulevard to rural coastal highway north of Nuevo Vallarta — repeated short, steep rollers through the Sierra de Vallejo foothills rather than one defining climb.
suffering_zones: Sierra de Vallejo coastal foothills between Higuera Blanca and Sayulita — the organizer's 'rolling terrain, short steep climbs' zone, no individual gradient data published
Open highway sections toward La Pena on the 130km Forte route — controlled but not fully closed circulation, real traffic-awareness required

logistics.airport: Puerto Vallarta International Airport (PVR) — roughly 30-40 minutes from the Nuevo Vallarta start/hub.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Official partner Marival Resorts offers a discount at Marival Emotions Resort & Suites (host hotel) or Marival Distinct Luxury Residences; abundant additional resort lodging throughout the Nuevo Vallarta/Bahia de Banderas corridor.

history.origin_story: Launched in 2025 as the current, RBC GranFondo/Whistler-team-backed edition of a Riviera Nayarit gran fondo, locally produced with Sabino Creaciones. This is a distinct, more recent iteration from an earlier same-region gran fondo documented in 2016 (a 165km, nine-climb, 1,800m course) — the organizer treats 2025 as the inaugural edition of the current event, not a continuation of that older race.
notable_moments: 2025: inaugural edition of the current RBC GranFondo-backed event
2026: second edition, 400-500 riders from Mexico, Canada, the US, Colombia and Spain
history.reputation: A young destination fondo backed by the RBC GranFondo/Whistler organizing team, with an international field (Mexico, Canada, US, Colombia, Spain per Nayarit tourism-board reporting) for a second-year event, but not yet a long-track-record or major-field classic. Its reputation currently rests on the organizing team's experience and the resort/coastal setting rather than longevity or competitive depth.

biased_opinion.verdict: A Young, Well-Backed Destination Fondo — Not Yet a Classic
biased_opinion.summary: GranFondo Riviera Nayarit is an active destination event, run by the RBC GranFondo/Whistler team with local production by Sabino Creaciones. Its 2026 (second) edition drew a producer-reported 400-500 riders from at least five countries per Nayarit tourism-board coverage — no independently audited starter/finisher count was found. The course is a coastal-highway route through the Sierra de Vallejo foothills north of Nuevo Vallarta, not the mountain-gran-fondo profile our old data (borrowed from a 2016 predecessor event) implied: a resort-corridor fondo with rolling terrain and short climbs, not a closed-highway mountain classic. Roads aren't fully closed to traffic either — a 2026 municipal notice describes controlled circulation with only a temporary closure on one boulevard.
strengths: Genuinely active with a real, confirmed 2026 edition — published results, organizer statements, and local/tourism-board coverage all agree
Backed by the experienced RBC GranFondo/Whistler organizing team, with professional local production
Easy resort-corridor logistics — 30-40 minutes from Puerto Vallarta International Airport, with abundant partner lodging
Growing international draw for a second-year event — riders from Mexico, Canada, the US, Colombia and Spain in 2026
weaknesses: Only two editions deep (2025-2026) under the current organizer — no long track record, and the field size (400-500) is modest
Roads are not fully closed — 2026 municipal notices describe controlled circulation with only a temporary closure on one boulevard, a real step down from a closed-highway event
Rolling coastal terrain with short climbs, not a marquee mountain ascent — riders expecting Alpine-scale climbing will be disappointed
No official source names a headline climb or publishes individual gradient data
Our own prior data conflated this event with an unrelated 2016 predecessor (165km/9 climbs/1,800m) — a different course under a similar regional banner
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A legitimate destination fondo for riders who want an easy-access resort trip with a supported ride attached — not a mountain-climbing classic. Go in with rolling-terrain, moderate-suffering expectations.

final_verdict.one_liner: A young, well-backed Pacific-coast destination fondo — easy resort access, rolling terrain, not a mountain classic.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want an easy-logistics resort trip with a genuine supported ride and international field. Skip it if you're chasing serious elevation or a closed-highway race atmosphere.
final_verdict.alternatives: For closed-highway mountain climbing from the same RBC GranFondo organizing team, see RBC GranFondo Whistler (Canada) directly. For a more established Mexican road gran fondo, research options with a longer track record than this two-edition event.

eligibility.notes: Successfully held Saturday, April 18, 2026 — the second edition of the current RBC GranFondo-backed event (first edition April 5, 2025), with published results and post-event coverage. No discontinuation signal found, but a 2027 edition has not yet been officially confirmed. VITALS CORRECTION: real 2026 courses are Forte 130km/1,120m and Gran Fondo 92km/980m; the old 140km figure and the 2016-video-sourced '165km/9 climbs/1,800m' course description both describe a different, earlier same-region event, not the current one — the organizer treats 2025 as this event's own inaugural edition.
